primarySong.title: Bésame Mucho
alphabetFeatures: Pure vowels: a, e, i, o, u all heard clearly in 'bésame' and 'mucho'
Soft B/V demonstration in 'bésame' — both letters share the same gentle bilabial sound
Stress mark on é teaches the role of Spanish accents (BÉ-sa-me)
Slow bolero tempo — ideal for first-listen phonetic parsing
Three-syllable target words easy to count and clap
primarySong.copyrightSafeNotes: Only individual letter examples ('bésame', 'mucho', 'hola', 'llorar', 'hablar') and the 2-word public title 'Bésame Mucho' may be cited as concept evidence. Never reproduce the chorus or any full lyric line. Quiz items must test Spanish alphabet/pronunciation (e.g., 'How is Spanish U pronounced?'), never lyric recall. The reading passage in modules.ts is original biographical content about Consuelo Velázquez and is copyright-safe.
primarySong.verificationNotes: YouTube ID MY0fuEfBmD4 confirmed via search results — labeled 'Consuelo Velazquez — Besame Mucho'. Spotify ID 6jXSjt2gZkLQ9yEAGOC7CR did NOT appear in any search result; the canonical Consuelo Velázquez recording surfaced in searches is 51RQwCpw50qKCpc0WBY49v. WebFetch was blocked, so Reine MUST spot-check the current Spotify ID by opening https://open.spotify.com/track/6jXSjt2gZkLQ9yEAGOC7CR and confirming title + artist. If wrong, swap to 51RQwCpw50qKCpc0WBY49v.

genreAlternatives.title: Limón y Sal
alphabetFeatures: Crystal-clear Mexican enunciation — Julieta Venegas is famous for crisp diction
All five pure vowels appear in the title alone (i, ó, y, a)
Silent H demonstration available in vocabulary tie-ins
Mid-tempo acoustic pop — accordion-driven, no autotune or vocal effects
Repetitive, predictable phrasing helps beginner ears latch on
genreAlternatives.notes: Strongest pop alternative for an A1 pronunciation module. Julieta Venegas's diction is benchmark-clean — language teachers routinely cite her catalog. Mid-tempo, no slang, family-friendly love song, culturally significant (Mexico).
genreAlternatives.copyrightSafeNotes: Cite only individual words (limón, sal, yo, te, quiero) and the 2-word title as alphabet/pronunciation evidence. Do not reproduce the chorus.

genreAlternatives.title: Despacito
alphabetFeatures: Title 'des-pa-ci-to' is itself a four-syllable pronunciation exercise — perfect for vowel/syllable lessons
Soft C before i in 'despacito' and 'suavecito' demonstrates the seseo/ceceo split
Diminutive -ito suffix shows pure -i-o vowel sequence
Most globally recognized Spanish-language song — students arrive with the melody already memorized
Mid-tempo reggaeton (~89 BPM) — slow for the genre, comprehensible to A1 ears
genreAlternatives.notes: By far the most beginner-friendly reggaeton track. Used in Module 4 as the reggaeton alt already — reuse is fine here because the pedagogical purpose is different (alphabet/pronunciation vs. prepositions). Contains some romantic/flirtation content but no profanity. Daddy Yankee's verse is faster — Reine may want to flag a 'focus on the chorus' note in the listen screen.
genreAlternatives.copyrightSafeNotes: Cite only the title (publicly available metadata) and individual phonetic targets ('des-pa-ci-to', 'sua-ve-ci-to') as syllabification examples. Do not reproduce any lyric line beyond 2-3 word phonetic chunks used for syllable-counting drills.

genreAlternatives.title: Burbujas de Amor
alphabetFeatures: Juan Luis Guerra's diction is conservatory-precise — Berklee-trained, sings every syllable cleanly
Rolled RR explicitly featured in 'burbujas' — perfect for the trilled-R lesson point
Silent H demo via 'amor' (no H) and contrast with potential H words
Soft, slow bachata tempo (~110 BPM) — gentle guitar texture, no vocal effects
All five vowels heard clearly across the title 'Burbujas de Amor'
genreAlternatives.notes: Highest concept-match score of all four genre alternatives. Bachata is the closest Latin-music analogue to R&B/soul (intimate, romantic, vocal-forward), and Juan Luis Guerra is its most pronunciation-clean exponent. Dominican Spanish but with very standard enunciation — no regional swallowing of consonants. Family-friendly romantic theme, no slang, no explicit content.
genreAlternatives.copyrightSafeNotes: Cite individual words (burbujas, amor, pez, ciudad) as alphabet/pronunciation evidence. The 3-word title 'Burbujas de Amor' is publicly available metadata. Do not reproduce the chorus.

genreAlternatives.title: Cielito Lindo
alphabetFeatures: THE archetypal Spanish vowel showcase — the famous 'Ay, ay, ay, ay' chorus is literally a pure-vowel drill set to music
Soft C before i in 'cielito' demonstrates pronunciation rule
Silent H absent here, but the LL in 'cielito' models the 'y'-like consonant sound
Canta y no llores — natural showcase of LL (llores) without needing to quote the full line
Mariachi instrumentation is slow, traditional, and crystal-clear; Vicente Fernández's enunciation is iconic
genreAlternatives.notes: Replaces 'La Bamba' by Los Lobos (which was wrongly duplicated across reggaeton and regional-mexican slots, and is rock/Chicano rather than regional Mexican). 'Cielito Lindo' is THE most internationally recognized Mexican folk song — every Spanish learner has heard the 'Ay, ay, ay, ay' chorus. Vicente Fernández's recording is the canonical mariachi version. Family-friendly, slow tempo, exceptionally clear pronunciation.
genreAlternatives.copyrightSafeNotes: The 'Ay, ay, ay, ay' interjection is in the public domain (Quirino Mendoza y Cortés, 1882) and is itself a vowel-pronunciation drill. Vicente Fernández's recording is copyrighted as a sound recording, but we are deep-linking only — no embedded playback. Cite individual words (cielito, lindo, canta) and the 2-word title as concept evidence. Do not reproduce verses.

rejectedCandidates.title: La Bamba
rejectedCandidates.reason: Currently duplicated in both the reggaeton and regional-mexican slots in modules.ts. Also miscategorized: 'La Bamba' is Chicano rock/Tex-Mex, not reggaeton and not traditional regional Mexican. The song is fast, slurred in places, and not the strongest fit for a pronunciation-focused A1 module. Replaced by Despacito (reggaeton) and Cielito Lindo (regional Mexican).

rejectedCandidates.title: Dákiti
rejectedCandidates.reason: Heavy autotune obscures pronunciation — fatal for an alphabet module. Also features Puerto Rican slang and lifestyle references that aren't A1-appropriate.

rejectedCandidates.title: Callaíta
rejectedCandidates.reason: Trap-influenced delivery, swallowed consonants typical of Puerto Rican urban Spanish — not the clean enunciation an alphabet/pronunciation module needs.
